questions.text: NAD is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mononucleotide consists of only one nucleotide (e.g., AMP – Adenosine Monophosphate), which NAD is not.
NAD (Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide) consists of two nucleotides joined by a phosphate group. One nucleotide contains adenine, and the other contains nicotinamide.
Trinucleotide consists of three nucleotides, which is not the case for NAD.
Tetranucleotide consists of four nucleotides, which is incorrect for NAD.

questions.explanation: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) is a cofactor central to metabolism. Found in all living cells, NAD is called a dinucleotide because it consists of two nucleotides joined through their phosphate groups. The cellular respiration processes of all living cells make use of the coenzyme Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD). It plays a key role in energy metabolism by accepting and donating electrons. The low energy form NAD+, shown at left, is raised to the high energy form 'NADH'.

questions.text: A group of biologically active molecules formed from amino acids which interact with the surface of the lipid bilayer of cell membranes are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Integral proteins are proteins that are permanently embedded in a cell's membrane. They are essential for life and perform many functions, including transporting molecules, generating energy, and transducing signals.
Peripheral proteins are a group of proteins that attach to the surface of a cell membrane. They are also known as peripheral membrane proteins or extrinsic membrane proteins.
A cell wall is a rigid or flexible layer that surrounds some cells, providing structure, protection, and shape. Cell walls are found in bacteria, fungi, algae, and plants.
Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that connect plant and some algal cells. They allow for communication and transport between cells.

questions.explanation: Peripheral proteins, or peripheral membrane proteins, are a group of biologically active molecules formed from amino acids which interact with the surface of the lipid bilayer of cell membranes. Unlike integral membrane proteins, peripheral proteins do not enter into the hydrophobic space within the cell membrane. Instead, peripheral proteins have specific sequences of amino acids which allow them to attract to the phosphate heads of the lipid molecules or to integral proteins.

questions.text: Which type of protein structure contains the three dimensional structure?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The primary structure is a sequence of amino acids in the protein/polypeptide. The secondary structure is a 2D structure. Tertiary structure is a 3D structure. Quaternary structure is when two or more 3D structures join together.

questions.text: What type of atom is the carbon atom?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Divalent atoms have two valence electrons in their outermost shell, allowing them to form two bonds. Examples include oxygen or sulfur.
Monovalent atoms have one valence electron in their outermost shell, enabling them to form one bond. Examples include hydrogen or lithium.
Trivalent atoms have three valence electrons in their outermost shell, allowing them to form three bonds. Examples include boron or nitrogen.

The carbon atom is tetravalent because it has four valence electrons in its outermost shell, allowing it to form four covalent bonds. This property is fundamental to its role in forming complex organic molecules.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Tetravalent. Carbon is tetravalent because it has four valence electrons in its outermost shell, which enables it to form four covalent bonds with other atoms. This valency allows carbon to be the backbone of organic molecules, forming stable and complex structures. The other options are incorrect: divalent atoms have two valence electrons, monovalent atoms have one, and trivalent atoms have three, none of which describe the valency of carbon.

questions.text: Which of the following fatty acids is least likely to be found in waxes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stearic acid is a saturated fatty acid found in various animal and vegetable fats. It is commonly used in the production of soaps, candles, and cosmetics. In the body, stearic acid can be synthesized from dietary fats and is a component of triglycerides.

Acetic acid is a simple carboxylic acid, a key component of vinegar, and is involved in various biological processes. It is commonly used in food preservation and as a condiment. In cellular metabolism, acetic acid is an intermediate in the conversion of sugars and fats into energy.

Oleic acid is a monounsaturated fatty acid found in various oils and fats, such as olive oil. It is beneficial for heart health and is a component of cell membranes. Oleic acid has anti-inflammatory properties and is associated with various health benefits.

Palmitic acid is a saturated fatty acid found in various animal and plant fats. It is a major component of palm oil and is involved in the synthesis of lipids. Excessive consumption of palmitic acid has been linked to negative health effects, particularly concerning cardiovascular health.

questions.explanation: Explanation:Chemically, waxes are mixtures of long chain alkanes with an odd number of carbon atoms ranging from C 25 to C35) and alcohols, ketones and esters of long chain fully acids.

questions.text: One of the most important molecule found in living organisms is ATP. What is its major function?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is one of the correct options.ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is a molecule that serves as the primary energy source for most cellular processes. It is produced by cells through the process of cellular respiration, which involves the breakdown of glucose and other molecules to release energy. ATP is used to power a wide variety of cellular processes, including muscle contraction, protein synthesis, and the transport of molecules across cell membranes. When ATP is used to power a cellular process, it is broken down into ADP (adenosine diphosphate) and inorganic phosphate, and the energy released is used to drive the process. ATP is then regenerated through the process of cellular respiration so that it can be used again to power other processes

It is one of the correct options.ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is also considered a coenzyme, which means that it is a molecule that is required for the activity of certain enzymes. Many enzymes require ATP in order to function properly, and ATP can help to facilitate the transfer of energy between different biochemical reactions. In addition, ATP can also act as a signal molecule within cells, helping to regulate a wide variety of cellular processes.

ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is not typically considered a cofactor, which is a molecule that is required for the activity of certain enzymes. Cofactors are often inorganic ions or small organic molecules that are bound to the enzyme and help to facilitate its activity. While ATP is involved in many cellular processes and can help to power the activity of certain enzymes, it is not usually considered a cofactor because it is not directly bound to the enzyme and does not participate in the chemical reaction catalyzed by the enzyme.

questions.text: When the glucose level in blood comes down, glucose is synthesized from
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While fats can be converted into glucose through gluconeogenesis, this is not the primary response to low blood glucose levels. This process is slower and more energy-intensive compared to glycogen breakdown.

Glycogen is the primary storage form of glucose in animals. When blood glucose levels decrease, glycogen is rapidly broken down into glucose in a process called glycogenolysis, providing a quick source of energy.

Amino acids can be converted into glucose via gluconeogenesis, but this is not the primary method for increasing blood glucose levels. It serves as a secondary mechanism when glycogen stores are depleted.
DNA does not serve as a source of glucose. It is involved in genetic information storage and protein synthesis, not energy production or regulation.

questions.explanation: Glycogen is the stored form of glucose in animals and serves as the primary rapid source of glucose when blood glucose levels are low. It is broken down into glucose through glycogenolysis, providing a quick energy supply. While fats and amino acids can also be converted into glucose, these processes are less immediate and are secondary responses. DNA does not play any role in glucose metabolism or regulation.

questions.text: The property of water due to which it works as a temperature stabilizer and hence protect living organisms from sudden thermal changes is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The dipole nature of water refers to its polarity, which is important for various properties of water, but it is not directly related to its role as a temperature stabilizer.

High specific heat of vaporization: While water does have a high specific heat of vaporization, which is the amount of heat energy required to convert a given quantity of liquid water into vapour at a constant temperature, this property is not specifically associated with its role as a temperature stabilizer.
Due to high specific heat capacity, water does not heat up easily and does not loose heat easily, as a result the temperature remains constant and the organisms don’t have to undergo changes.

Water being in its liquid state is essential for its various properties and functions, including its role as a temperature stabilizer. However, the specific property that enables water to work as a temperature stabilizer is its high specific heat capacity.

questions.explanation: Due to high specific heat capacity, water does not heat up easily and does not loose heat easily, as a result the temperature remains constant and the organisms don’t have to undergo changes.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most complex sugar?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monosaccharides are the simplest carbohydrates; they conform to the general chemical formula (CH2O)x and are termed simple sugars. The most commonly occurring monosaccharides contain three to six carbon atoms in an unbranched single-bonded chain. Monosaccharides are signified by the suffix -ose.

Oligosaccharides represent carbohydrates that contain between 3 and 10 single sugar residues and are not relatively abundant in the diet when compared to other more common carbohydrates like those in the disaccharide category. Common oligosaccharides include raffinose, stachyose, and verbascose.

Polysaccharides are major classes of biomolecules. They are long chains of carbohydrate molecules, composed of several smaller monosaccharides. These complex bio-macromolecules functions as an important source of energy in animal cell and form a structural component of a plant cell.

Carbohydrates, or carbs, are sugar molecules. Along with proteins and fats, carbohydrates are one of three main nutrients found in foods and drinks. Your body breaks down carbohydrates into glucose. Glucose, or blood sugar, is the main source of energy for your body's cells, tissues, and organs.

questions.explanation: Polysaccharides are major classes of biomolecules. They are long chains of carbohydrate molecules, composed of several smaller monosaccharides. These complex bio-macromolecules functions as an important source of energy in animal cell and form a structural component of a plant cell. Oligosaccharides are carbohydrates with 3 to 10 subunits. They are simpler than polysaccharides but more complex than mono (1 unit) and disacccharides(2 units).

questions.text: 'Biomolecules' such as starch, cellulose and glycogen yield which of the following upon hydrolysis?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Maltose: Maltose is a disaccharide composed of two glucose molecules linked together. It is formed by the hydrolysis of starch or glycogen. So, it does not yield maltose upon hydrolysis.

b) Sucrose: Sucrose is also a disaccharide composed of one glucose molecule and one fructose molecule linked together. It is commonly found in table sugar. The hydrolysis of biomolecules like starch, cellulose, and glycogen does not yield sucrose.

c) Glucose: Glucose is a monosaccharide and the simplest form of sugar. It is the main product obtained upon the hydrolysis of starch, cellulose, and glycogen. These biomolecules are polymers of glucose units, and when they undergo hydrolysis, they break down into individual glucose molecules.

d) Galactose: Galactose is another monosaccharide, similar to glucose. It is not the primary product obtained from the hydrolysis of starch, cellulose, and glycogen. Instead, glucose is the main product.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct since the three most abundant polysaccharides, starch, glycogen, and cellulose ( referred to as homopolymers) yield only one type of monosaccharide i.e. 'glucose' after complete hydrolysis.

questions.text: What are the distinguishing features of fibrous proteins?

options.explanation: Elastic is partially correct because some fibrous proteins, such as elastin, confer elasticity to tissues, allowing them to stretch and recoil.
Non-crystalline is partially correct because fibrous proteins do not form crystalline structures. Instead, they are elongated and filamentous.

questions.text: The number of calories required to raise the temperature of 1g of water from 15 °C to 16 °C is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Specific heat of vaporization: This option is incorrect. The specific heat of vaporizationrefers to the amount of heat energy required to change the state of a substance from liquid togas at a constant temperature and pressure. It is not relevant to the scenario described, whichinvolves raising the temperature of water by 1°C.

The correct answer is "specific heat capacity," because it refers to the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a substance by a certain amount. It is a property that quantifies the ability of a substance to store or release heat energy. Specific heat capacity is expressed in units of calories per gram per degree Celsius (cal/g°C) or joules per kilogram per degree Celsius (J/kg°C) in the metric system.

questions.text: What is the role of cholesterol in the cell membrane?

options.text: It functions in joining adjacent cells together

options.text: It functions in recognizing foreign cells
options.explanation: T cells only recognize foreign antigens that are displayed on the surfaces of the body's own cells.

options.text: It increases the rate of transport of ions across the membrane
options.explanation: Many stimuli increase the rate of ions flowing across or into the cell membrane. The main types of stimuli that are known to cause ion channels to open are a change in the voltage across the membrane (voltage-gated channels), a mechanical stress (mechanically gated channels), or the binding of a ligand (ligand-gated channels).
Cholesterol is a lipid molecule that is present in the lipid bilayer of the cell membrane.

questions.explanation: 'Cholesterol' is interspersed between the phospholipid bilayer. In case of temperature deviation, the fatty acid tails may melt or freeze. Changing the fluidity of the membrane, that is, with an increase in temperature, the phospholipid entities tend to move away from each other, and at low temperatures, the molecules will move very close to each other, disturbing the membrane's ability to transport substances. Cholesterol is the stabilizing molecule that prevents extreme movement of phospholipids during temperature fluctuationSo, the correct answer is 'It stabilizes the cell membrane.'

questions.text: Lysosomes function in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Protein synthesis occurs in the ribosomes, not in lysosomes. Ribosomes translate genetic information into proteins, a process separate from the digestive function of lysosomes.
While lysosomes process materials by breaking them down, their primary function is to degrade and recycle cellular waste, rather than processing it in a constructive manner.
Lysosomes act as the cell's digestive system, breaking down materials from both inside and outside the cell and recycling cellular components.
Lipid synthesis occurs in the endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi apparatus, not in lysosomes which are dedicated to digestion and recycling.
Carbohydrate synthesis is carried out by specific enzymes in the cytoplasm, while lysosomes focus on breaking down and recycling cellular waste.

questions.explanation: Lysosomes are specialized organelles within the cell that contain hydrolytic enzymes capable of breaking down macromolecules like proteins, nucleic acids, lipids, and carbohydrates. Their primary function is intracellular digestion, where they degrade and recycle cellular waste, damaged organelles, and material ingested by the cell. Unlike ribosomes, lysosomes do not synthesize proteins, and they are not involved in lipid or carbohydrate synthesis, which occur in other organelles. Options such as 'protein synthesis', 'lipid synthesis', and 'carbohydrate synthesis' refer to processes that take place outside of lysosomes, highlighting their specialized role in cellular maintenance through digestion.

questions.text: What type of virus is the smallpox virus?

options.explanation: These viruses have a DNA genome surrounded by a capsid and an envelope. They often establish persistent infections.Examples: Herpes simplex virus (Herpesviridae), Hepatitis B virus (Hepadnaviridae), Poxvirus (Poxviridae), Cytomegalovirus (Herpesviridae).

questions.explanation: Viruses are classified by genome type and presence of an envelope. DNA-enveloped viruses (e.g., herpes, hepatitis B, and poxvirus) contain a large double-stranded DNA genome with both capsid and envelope, whereas DNA-non-enveloped viruses (e.g., adenovirus and papillomavirus) package DNA only in a protein shell. questions.text: The viruses are:

options.explanation: Viruses are not considered cellular organisms because they lack structures essential to cells, such as a cell membrane and organelles. They cannot independently carry out life processes like metabolism or reproduction.

Eukaryotes have complex cells with a nucleus and organelles. Viruses lack these cellular components and cannot perform the functions of eukaryotic cells, such as energy production and reproduction.

Viruses are extremely small, typically ranging from 20 to 300 nanometers, which is far too small to be seen without a microscope. Observing viruses requires advanced techniques like electron microscopy.

questions.explanation: Viruses are classified as non-cellular because they lack the cellular structures that define living cells, such as a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and organelles. Instead, they consist of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat called a capsid. Unlike cellular organisms, viruses cannot perform metabolic processes or reproduce on their own; they must infect a host cell to replicate. This distinguishes them from cellular life forms, including prokaryotes and eukaryotes.Option A (Cellular) is incorrect because viruses do not have the structures that define cellular life. Option B (Prokaryotes) is incorrect because viruses do not fit the definition of prokaryotic cells, which have a cell membrane and cytoplasm. questions.text: Which of the following is the simplest form of pathogens causing diseases?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses, while simple compared to many other life forms, still consist of genetic material (DNA or RNA) encapsulated in a protein coat. They require a host cell to reproduce and cannot independently cause disease, making them more complex than prions.

Prions are infectious agents that consist solely of misfolded proteins without any nucleic acid. They are considered the simplest form of pathogens because they lack cellular structure and replication machinery, which distinguishes them from other pathogens.

Fungi are complex organisms with defined cellular structures and reproductive mechanisms. They require specific environmental conditions to grow and are not the simplest pathogens, as they are multicellular or unicellular with extensive biological functions.

Amoebas are single-celled organisms that can cause diseases, but they are more complex than prions, as they possess cellular structures, organelles, and various pathogenic mechanisms. Thus, they do not represent the simplest form of pathogens.

questions.explanation: Prions are the simplest disease-causing pathogens because they are merely misfolded proteins with with no nucleic acid (no DNA or RNA), making them the most minimal infectious agents compared to viruses, fungi, or protozoa like amoeba.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a viral disease?

options.explanation: AIDS is a viral illness that develops when a person is infected with the human immunodeficiency virus, or HIV. This virus weakens the immune system over time, making the body more vulnerable to infections and certain cancers.
Malaria is not a viral illness. It’s caused by protozoa of the genus Plasmodium, which are transmitted to humans through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.
Influenza is a viral infection, and it’s caused by the influenza virus, which mainly affects the respiratory tract.
Chickenpox is a viral infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus. It typically leads to an itchy, blister-like rash along with fever and fatigue.

questions.explanation: Malaria is not a viral disease and is caused by the protozoan Plasmodium. All other options are viral infections that include AIDS caused by the HIV virus, influenza caused by the influenza virus, chickenpox caused by the varicella-zoster virus (VZV), and rabies caused by the rabies lyssavirus.

questions.text: Which of the following is not related to enveloped viruses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: They survive for a short time.
options.explanation: Enveloped viruses survive for a short period of time because their lipid envelope is fragile and easily damaged by heat, drying, detergents, and disinfectants. Once the envelope is disrupted, the virus can no longer attach to or enter host cells, making it non-infectious. In contrast, non-enveloped (naked) viruses lack this lipid layer and are more resistant, allowing them to survive longer in harsh environmental conditions.
It is incorrect. Enveloped viruses are less resistant to antibodies or disinfectants and can be easily destroyed.

options.text: Their envelope is sensitive to sunlight.
options.explanation: Enveloped viruses are sensitive to sunlight, temperature, and pH because their outer lipid envelope is delicate and unstable. Ultraviolet (UV) rays from sunlight can damage both the lipids and viral nucleic acid, high temperatures can denature the envelope proteins needed for attachment, and extreme pH can disrupt the lipid bilayer and protein structure. Since the envelope is essential for host cell entry, any damage to it makes the virus non-infectious. This is why enveloped viruses are less stable in the environment compared to non-enveloped viruses.
It is true. The envelope is derived from the host by the process of budding.

questions.explanation: Enveloped viruses are less resistant to antibodies or disinfectants and can be easily destroyed. The envelope is derived from the host and is sensitive to sunlight. They survive for a short time.

questions.text: Numerous opportunistic diseases can affect individuals with the following conditions.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Measles weakens the immune system, making individuals more susceptible to opportunistic infections. While measles itself is not an opportunistic infection, it can compromise the immune system, paving the way for other diseases.

Influenza, commonly known as the flu, can also temporarily weaken the immune system. Similar to measles, influenza doesn't directly lead to opportunistic infections but can make individuals more vulnerable to other diseases during the weakened state.

Hepatitis A primarily affects the liver but is not considered an opportunistic infection. Like measles and influenza, hepatitis A may weaken the immune system, potentially allowing opportunistic infections to take hold.

AIDS is caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), which attacks the immune system, specifically CD4 cells (T cells), weakening the body's defense against infections. HIV/AIDS is associated with numerous opportunistic infections and diseases, including pneumonia, tuberculosis, and various fungal, bacterial, and viral infections.

questions.explanation: Among the given options, the correct answer is d. AIDS. Measles, influenza, and hepatitis A can temporarily weaken the immune system, increasing susceptibility to other infections, but they are not classified as opportunistic infections. In contrast, AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome), caused by HIV, leads to a profound loss of CD4⁺ T cells and severe immunodeficiency. This immunosuppressed state is specifically characterized by the occurrence of opportunistic infections such as tuberculosis, pneumonia, candidiasis, and other fungal, bacterial, and viral diseases. Thus, unlike measles, influenza, and hepatitis A, AIDS is directly associated with opportunistic infections due to its defining immune system damage.

The term virion refers to the complete, mature, and infectious virus particle. It includes the genetic material (genome), as well as the protein coat (capsid) that surrounds and protects the genetic material. The virion is the fully formed, functional virus particle that is capable of infecting host cells.

questions.text: For the synthesis of mRNA, HIV uses the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cytoplasmic RNA polymerases are enzymes that function in the cytoplasm of certain viruses, particularly large DNA viruses like poxviruses. These viruses carry or encode their own RNA polymerases, which allow them to transcribe their genes directly in the cytoplasm without depending on the host nucleus. However, HIV does not encode or use such a cytoplasmic RNA polymerase for mRNA synthesis.
This option would imply that HIV does not use any of the above-mentioned enzymes, which is incorrect. HIV clearly uses the host RNA polymerase II for its transcriptional needs.

Host RNA polymerases are enzymes naturally present in eukaryotic cells to carry out transcription. In the case of HIV, after the viral RNA genome is reverse-transcribed into DNA by the viral enzyme reverse transcriptase, this DNA integrates into the host genome. Once integrated, HIV relies on the host cell’s RNA polymerase II to transcribe its genes into mRNA, which can then be translated into viral proteins. Thus, HIV hijacks the host RNA polymerase system.

Some viruses, such as influenza virus or other RNA viruses, encode their own RNA-dependent RNA polymerases, which they use to transcribe their genomes directly into mRNA. However, HIV does not carry a viral RNA polymerase for mRNA synthesis. Instead, its only major polymerase activity is reverse transcriptase, which makes DNA from RNA, not mRNA from DNA. Therefore, this option is incorrect for HIV.

questions.explanation: The synthesis of HIV mRNA does not occur through a cytoplasmic RNA polymerase or a viral RNA polymerase. Instead, HIV depends entirely on the host’s RNA polymerase II after integration of its DNA into the host genome. Therefore, the correct option is Host RNA polymerase.

questions.text: Viruses do not have:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: There is no physical 'fossil record' of viruses; virions persist for short time periods and rapidly degrade leaving no direct trace of their existence.

The origins of viruses in the evolutionary history of life are still unclear. Some viruses may have evolved from plasmids, which are pieces of DNA that can move between cells. Other viruses may have evolved from bacteria.

A virus is a tiny, infectious particle that can reproduce only by infecting a host cell. Viruses "commandeer" the host cell and use its resources to make more viruses, basically reprogramming it to become a virus factory. Because they can't reproduce by themselves (without a host), viruses are not considered living.

The diversity of nucleic acids found in viruses is startling. Viruses may contain linear double-stranded (ds) DNAs; circular ds DNAs; linear or circular single-stranded (ss) DNAs; coding ('+ strand') RNAs; RNAs ('− strand') whose complements encode viral proteins; and dsRNAs.

questions.explanation: Viruses do not have a fossil record because they lack hard structures such as bones, shells, or cell walls that can be preserved over time. They are made up only of nucleic acids and protein coats, which are too small and fragile to leave traces in rocks or sediments. In addition, their tiny size and dependence on living cells make it nearly impossible for them to be fossilized. Instead of fossils, scientists study the evolutionary history of viruses through their genetic material and by comparing it with that of their hosts.

questions.text: Viruses contain:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: DNA: Some viruses have DNA as their genetic material. These viruses are called DNA viruses. Their genome consists of double-stranded DNA molecules, similar to the genetic material found in cells. Examples of DNA viruses include herpesviruses, adenoviruses, and poxviruses.

RNA: Other viruses have RNA as their genetic material. These viruses are called RNA viruses. Their genome consists of single-stranded RNA molecules. RNA viruses can be further categorized into two types:a. Positive-sense RNA: These viruses have RNA genomes that can act as messenger RNA (mRNA) directly upon infection. Examples of positive-sense RNA viruses include coronaviruses, influenza viruses, and hepatitis C virus.b. Negative-sense RNA: These viruses have RNA genomes that are complementary to the viral mRNA. They must first transcribe their RNA into mRNA before protein synthesis can occur. Examples of negative-sense RNA viruses include measles virus, Ebola virus, and rabies virus.

RNA or DNA: There are some viruses that can have either RNA or DNA as their genetic material. For example, retroviruses, such as HIV, initially have an RNA genome but reverse transcribe it into DNA upon infection. The DNA form then integrates into the host cell's genome.
RNA and DNA: It is not common for viruses to have both RNA and DNA as their genetic material. Viruses usually have either RNA or DNA, but not both simultaneously.

options.explanation: The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill the lowest energy levels first before moving to higher energy levels. However, it does not explain why nitrogen and phosphorus specifically have three unpaired valence electrons.

Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that it is impossible to simultaneously determine the exact position and momentum of an electron. This principle is not directly related to the presence of unpaired valence electrons in nitrogen and phosphorus.

Hund's rule is the correct explanation for why nitrogen and phosphorus have three unpaired valence electrons. According to Hund's rule, when filling orbitals of equal energy (such as the p orbitals of nitrogen and phosphorus), electrons occupy separate orbitals with parallel spins before pairing up. This results in three unpaired electrons in the valence shell of nitrogen and phosphorus, which maximizes the electron-electron repulsion and stabilizes the atom.
It is unclear what "Planck's statement" refers to. If it is referring to Planck's quantum theory, it does not directly explain the presence of unpaired valence electrons in nitrogen and phosphorus.

questions.explanation: The valency of an atom will be one if there is a sufficient gap between the first and second ionization energies. Ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from an atom in its gaseous state. The first ionization energy is the energy required to remove the first electron from the atom, and the second ionization energy is the energy required to remove the second electron from the atom.If there is a large gap between the first and second ionization energies, it means that it is much more difficult to remove the second electron than the first electron. This is because the first electron is loosely bound to the atom, but the second electron is more tightly bound.An atom with a large gap between the first and second ionization energies will tend to have a valency of one. This is because the atom will be more likely to lose one electron than two electrons.So the answer is ionization energy.

questions.text: Sigma bond is formed by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because the transfer of electrons leads to the formation of ionic bonds, not sigma bonds.
Option B is correct. A sigma bond is characterized by the head-on overlapping of atomic orbitals, allowing for the strongest type of covalent bond.

options.text: Mutual but unequal sharing of electrons
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect because mutual but unequal sharing of electrons results in a polar covalent bond, not a sigma bond.
Option D is incorrect because parallel overlapping of atomic orbitals results in the formation of pi bonds, not sigma bonds.
Option E is incorrect since only option B describes the correct process for forming sigma bonds.

questions.explanation: The sigma bond, the strongest type of covalent bond, is formed by the head-on overlapping of atomic orbitals. This overlap allows for maximum electron density between the two bonding nuclei, resulting in a stable bond. In contrast, ionic bonds are formed by the transfer of electrons (Option A), polar covalent bonds arise from mutual but unequal sharing of electrons (Option C), and pi bonds result from the parallel overlapping of atomic orbitals (Option D). Thus, Option B is the only correct description for sigma bond formation.

questions.explanation: Bond energy is a critical concept in understanding chemical bonding and is defined through multiple perspectives:Option I: It is indeed the energy required to break a bond between two atoms in a diatomic molecule, indicating how much energy is needed to overcome the bond's strength.Option II: The energy released when a bond is formed from free atoms is also a measure of bond energy, showing the stabilization energy provided during bond formation.Option III: Bond energy serves as an indicator of bond strength; stronger bonds require more energy to break, thus having higher bond energies.Therefore, all three options collectively provide a comprehensive understanding of bond energy, making Option E the correct choice. questions.text: sp3 hybridization in CH4 gives it _ geometry.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Tetrahedral. questions.text: GN Lewis described which one of the following bonds?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An ionic bond involves the transfer of electrons from one atom to another, resulting in the formation of ions. G.N. Lewis primarily focused on electron pair sharing in covalent bonds rather than ionic bonds.

G.N. Lewis is well-known for his description of covalent bonds, where atoms share electron pairs to attain stability. His work led to the concept of the Lewis structure, which represents covalent bonds.
A coordinate covalent bond, also known as a dative bond, is a type of covalent bond where both electrons in the shared pair come from the same atom. Lewis's theory also encompasses this type of bond.

G.N. Lewis is credited with the foundational concepts that describe covalent bonds, including ordinary covalent bonds and coordinate covalent bonds. He focused on electron pair sharing, which applies to all these bond types.

questions.explanation: G.N. Lewis is renowned for his work on the nature of chemical bonding, particularly the sharing of electron pairs between atoms, which forms the basis of covalent bonds. questions.text: The bonding pair of electrons are equally shared between the atoms in?

options.explanation: In hydrogen fluoride (HF), the bonding pair of electrons is not equally shared between the atoms. Fluorine is more electronegative than hydrogen, so it attracts the bonding electrons closer to itself, resulting in a polar covalent bond. As a result, HF has a partial positive charge on the hydrogen atom and a partial negative charge on the fluorine atom.

Similar to HF, in hydrogen chloride (HCl), the bonding pair of electrons is not equally shared between the atoms. Chlorine is more electronegative than hydrogen, leading to a polar covalent bond. HCl has a partial positive charge on the hydrogen atom and a partial negative charge on the chlorine atom.

In water (H2O), the bonding pair of electrons is also not equally shared between the atoms. Oxygen is more electronegative than hydrogen, causing the oxygen atom to pull the electrons towards itself, resulting in a polar covalent bond. Water has a partial positive charge on the hydrogen atoms and a partial negative charge on the oxygen atom.

In hydrogen gas (H2), the bonding pair of electrons is equally shared between the two hydrogen atoms. Both hydrogen atoms have the same electronegativity, so they share the electrons equally, forming a nonpolar covalent bond.

questions.text: Melting and boiling points depends on _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Melting and boiling points of substances depend on various factors, and one of the factors that play a role is the number of electrons. Electrons are negatively charged subatomic particles that surround the nucleus of an atom. In an atom or molecule, the arrangement of electrons and their energy levels influence the strength of intermolecular forces between particles.Substances with a larger number of electrons generally have stronger van der Waals forces (London dispersion forces) between molecules. These forces arise due to temporary fluctuations in electron distributions, resulting in temporary dipoles. More electrons mean larger temporary dipoles and stronger intermolecular forces, leading to higher melting and boiling points.

The number of unpaired electrons also affects the properties of substances, but it is not directly related to their melting and boiling points. Unpaired electrons are electrons that are not paired with another electron in the same orbital. They play a role in chemical bonding and reactivity, but they do not significantly influence the physical properties like melting and boiling points.

The number of paired electrons is not directly related to the melting and boiling points either. Paired electrons are electrons that are paired with another electron in the same orbital, and they do not significantly affect the physical properties of substances.

The number of protons in an atom determines its atomic number and chemical identity, but it does not directly affect the melting and boiling points of substances. The properties of substances are primarily influenced by the arrangement of electrons and the interactions between molecules.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not a state function?

Internal energy is the sum of all potential and kinetic energies of a system. It is also not possible to measure the internal energies of a system instead a change in internal energy is measured from change in state of system hence it is also a state function.

questions.text: Which of the following changes when a body performs uniform circular motion?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the speed changes when a body performs uniform circular motion. However, in uniform circular motion, the speed of the body remains constant. While the body changes direction continuously, the magnitude of its velocity (speed) remains the same.

This option suggests that the mass changes when a body performs uniform circular motion. However, in uniform circular motion, the mass of the body does not change. Mass is a scalar quantity that remains constant unless some external factors act on the body.

This option suggests that the direction changes when a body performs uniform circular motion. This statement is correct. In uniform circular motion, the body continuously changes its direction while maintaining a constant speed. The velocity vector changes direction at every point along the circular path, making the motion circular.

This option suggests that the kinetic energy changes when a body performs uniform circular motion. However, in uniform circular motion, the kinetic energy of the body remains constant. Kinetic energy is proportional to the square of the speed, and since the speed remains constant, the kinetic energy does not change.

questions.explanation: When a body performs uniform circular motion, the direction of its velocity continuously changes, while the speed (magnitude of velocity) remains constant. Therefore, the correct answer is option C: Direction. Options A, B, and D are incorrect as the speed, mass, and kinetic energy respectively do not change during uniform circular motion.
